And if I were to boil this all down to one thing, it would be skills. And I don't mean like human skills, I mean agent skills, which are these reusable instructions that give Claude competence. You can basically think of these as reusable and specialized prompts for Claude. However, instead of having to write and manage these yourself, they're baked directly into the Claude application. So, you don't have to manage all these things yourself. And so, why skills? Ultimately, the greatest value of skills is that they enable you to teach Claude how to do something one time and then it knows how to do that thing forever. So, it's kind of like hiring an employee that learns very quickly and never forgets anything. Another value of skills, it's fully integrated into the Claude interface. So, you don't have to have a bunch of like Google Docs or something like that and then have to switch between apps and copy-paste prompts when you're trying to do specific things. You just ask Claude to do something and it automatically knows what skill to use and pulls that into the chat. Another great thing about skills is that you can easily improve them and update them over time as you're having more conversations, as you're discovering new edge cases, as you want Claude to do more and more and take more and more off your plate. And finally, skills can be mixed and matched. You can have skills for different domains of your business. You can have skills for different types of tasks that you do. You can have skills for different tools that Claude has access to. And it's smart enough to know which skills are relevant to the ask and then pulls those in to the current session and uses them to get the work done. That's what skills are and why they're helpful. You can also have other things in this skill folder. You can have a bunch of subfolders with additional pieces of context or text files or screenshots or whatever else Claude needs to do good work. And a key feature of skills is this idea of so-called progressive disclosure. And that's just a fancy way of saying skills give Claude the right context at the right time. What I mean by this is at the start of any new session with Claude, all your available skills will be loaded into Claude's context window. So, Claude will be able to see all the names and short descriptions of every skill you have available. But this is basically how I've been onboarding Claude onto my team using these agent skills. So, the first step is you get Claude to do the task one time. This might be updating your CRM, doing pre-sales call research, or it could be taking a meeting transcript and turning it into action items and drafting a call follow-up to everyone who was on the meeting. So, basically anything, anything you're trying to get done, you can just get Claude to do it. So, what you'll find is there'll be a lot of back and forth in this process. Tell Claude to do something, it's going to try it, it might make mistakes, it might have to gather some context to better understand your business or the task or the tools or whatever it is. So, you're going to go through this iterative loop. And maybe like 5, 10, 15, even 30 minutes later, Claude has successfully done the task. So, that was a lot of work. But instead of doing this every single time, you can just do it once and then go to step two, which is to distill the conversation into a skill. So, basically just tell Claude, "Hey, reflect back on the conversation that we just had and come up with a plan to create a new skill for this task." Now, with this new skill, you won't have to go through this back and forth every single time. Claude should be able to grab the skill and do the task correctly on the first try. Once you've created the skill, you can test it. So, now Claude is equipped with this skill. You can give it a new ask. So, let's say you had it analyze a meeting transcript and then send a follow-up email. So, you'll give Claude the ask, it'll produce an answer, and then you can evaluate that answer. So, you can read it, you can see what it did well, can see what opportunities there are for improvement. And if it does anything wrong, this is actually a great thing because you can give Claude feedback and have it update the skill to avoid this mistake in the future. And so this fourth step is what enables Claude to get smarter every time you use it. Every time you're asking Claude to do a specific task and it's using a specific skill, anytime it makes a mistake, you can give it feedback and have it update the skill so it avoids that mistake in the future.
